title: Proton transfer reactions
summary: This lesson introduces the Brønsted-Lowry theory of acids and bases, defining them as proton donors and acceptors. You will learn to identify conjugate acid-base pairs in a reaction and understand the properties of amphiprotic species, which can act as either an acid or a base.
learningObjectives: Define a Brønsted-Lowry acid as a proton ($H^+$) donor and a Brønsted-Lowry base as a proton ($H^+$) acceptor.
Identify the acid, base, conjugate acid, and conjugate base in a proton transfer reaction.
Deduce the two conjugate acid-base pairs for a given reaction.
Define an amphiprotic species and provide examples such as water and the hydrogen carbonate ion.

simpleExplanation.title: The Proton Passing Game
simpleExplanation.summary: In chemistry, an acid is a substance that gives away a proton, and a base is one that accepts it. This simple act of passing a proton transforms the original acid and base into their 'conjugate' forms.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a dance where partners are constantly swapping a single hat (the proton). One person starts with the hat (the acid) and gives it to their partner (the base). After the swap, the first person is now ready to receive a hat (becoming a conjugate base), and their partner is now ready to give the hat away (becoming a conjugate acid). The entire reaction is just this continuous exchange.
steps: Identify the reactants. Look for a species that can donate a proton (the acid, e.g., HCl) and one that can accept it (the base, e.g., H₂O).
Visualise the proton transfer. The base uses a lone pair of electrons to form a new bond with the proton from the acid.
Determine the products. The acid, having lost a proton, becomes its conjugate base (e.g., Cl⁻). The base, having gained a proton, becomes its conjugate acid (e.g., H₃O⁺).
Pair them up. Connect the original acid with its conjugate base and the original base with its conjugate acid. These are the two conjugate pairs in the reaction.

flashcards.front: What is the definition of a Brønsted-Lowry acid?
What is the definition of a Brønsted-Lowry base?

flashcards.back: The species formed when a Brønsted-Lowry base accepts a proton.
The species formed when a Brønsted-Lowry acid donates a proton.

flashcards.front: What is a conjugate acid-base pair?
flashcards.back: Two species that differ from each other by a single proton ($H^+$).
A species that can act as either a Brønsted-Lowry acid (donating a proton) or a Brønsted-Lowry base (accepting a proton).

flashcards.front: Give two common examples of amphiprotic species.
flashcards.back: Water ($H_2O$) and the hydrogen carbonate ion ($HCO_3^-$).

flashcards.front: What is the conjugate acid of the base $HPO_4^{2-}$?
flashcards.back: $H_2PO_4^-$. (Accepting a proton, $H^+$, increases the charge by +1 and adds an H).

flashcards.front: What is the conjugate base of the acid $H_2S$?
flashcards.back: $HS^-$. (Donating a proton, $H^+$, decreases the charge by 1 and removes an H).

flashcards.front: Common Trap: What is the difference between 'amphiprotic' and 'amphoteric'?
flashcards.back: Amphiprotic specifically refers to donating/accepting protons (Brønsted-Lowry). Amphoteric is a broader term for any substance acting as an acid or base (including Lewis theory). All amphiprotic species are amphoteric, but not all amphoteric species are amphiprotic.

flashcards.front: Identify the Brønsted-Lowry base in the forward reaction: $CH_3COOH(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons CH_3COO^-(aq) + H_3O^+(aq)$
flashcards.back: $H_2O$, because it accepts a proton from $CH_3COOH$ to form $H_3O^+$.

faq.q: In the context of Brønsted-Lowry theory, is a proton the same as a hydrogen ion?
faq.a: Yes. A hydrogen atom ($^1H$) consists of one proton and one electron. When it loses this electron to form the $H^+$ ion, only the proton remains. So, in acid-base chemistry, 'proton' and 'hydrogen ion' are used interchangeably.

faq.q: Can a substance be a base if it doesn't contain hydroxide ($OH^-$)?
faq.a: Absolutely. This is a key advantage of the Brønsted-Lowry theory over the older Arrhenius theory. Any species that can accept a proton is a base. Ammonia ($NH_3$) is a classic example of a base that does not contain a hydroxide group in its formula.

faq.q: How do I identify the acid and base in a reaction just by looking at the equation?
faq.a: Look for changes between reactants and products. Find a reactant that has lost an $H^+$ on the product side – that's your acid. The reactant that has gained that $H^+$ is your base. For example, in $HNO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons NO_2^- + H_3O^+$, $HNO_2$ becomes $NO_2^-$ (lost $H^+$), so it's the acid. $H_2O$ becomes $H_3O^+$ (gained $H^+$), so it's the base.

sections.content: Welcome to the world of proton transfer! While you may have learned that acids produce $H^+$ ions in water, this is only part of the story. The Brønsted-Lowry theory provides a more powerful and general framework for understanding acid-base chemistry. It focuses on the simple act of a proton moving from one chemical species to another, a fundamental process that occurs in countless chemical and biological systems.

In 1923, Johannes Brønsted and Thomas Lowry independently proposed new definitions for acids and bases. Their theory is not restricted to aqueous solutions and provides a clearer picture of the role of the solvent. A Brønsted-Lowry acid is a species that donates a proton, and a Brønsted-Lowry base is a species that accepts a proton. A 'proton' in this context is simply a hydrogen ion, $H^+$, which is a hydrogen atom that has lost its electron.
Acid + Base \rightleftharpoons Conjugate Base + Conjugate Acid
Consider the reaction between hydrogen chloride gas and water. The HCl molecule donates a proton to a water molecule. Therefore, HCl is the acid and $H_2O$ is the base.
HCl(g) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons Cl^-(aq) + H_3O^+(aq) \\ \text{(Acid)} + \text{(Base)} \rightleftharpoons \text{(Conj. Base)} + \text{(Conj. Acid)}

A key consequence of the Brønsted-Lowry theory is the concept of conjugate pairs. When an acid donates its proton, the species remaining is called its conjugate base. When a base accepts a proton, the new species formed is its conjugate acid. Every Brønsted-Lowry acid-base reaction involves two such pairs.

items: A conjugate acid-base pair consists of two species that differ by a single proton ($H^+$).
In the reaction $HCl + H_2O$, the pairs are $HCl/Cl^-$ and $H_3O^+/H_2O$.

sections.question:
For the following reaction, identify the Brønsted-Lowry acid, base, conjugate acid, and conjugate base:

$NH_3(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons NH_4^+(aq) + OH^-(aq)$
sections.solution:
1.  **Analyse the reactants and products:** Compare the species on both sides of the equilibrium.
2.  **Track the proton:** The $NH_3$ molecule becomes $NH_4^+$. It has gained a hydrogen and its charge has increased by +1. Therefore, it has accepted a proton.
3.  **Identify the base and conjugate acid:** Since $NH_3$ accepts a proton, it is the Brønsted-Lowry base. The product, $NH_4^+$, is its conjugate acid.
4.  **Track the other species:** The $H_2O$ molecule becomes $OH^-$. It has lost a hydrogen and its charge has decreased by 1. Therefore, it has donated a proton.
5.  **Identify the acid and conjugate base:** Since $H_2O$ donates a proton, it is the Brønsted-Lowry acid. The product, $OH^-$, is its conjugate base.

**Answer according to markscheme:**
*   Acid: $H_2O$ [1]
*   Base: $NH_3$ [1]
*   Conjugate Acid: $NH_4^+$ [1]
*   Conjugate Base: $OH^-$ [1]

*(Note: Often, identifying both pairs correctly, e.g., $H_2O/OH^-$ and $NH_3/NH_4^+$, would gain the marks.)*

sections.content: Some substances have the remarkable ability to act as either a Brønsted-Lowry acid or a Brønsted-Lowry base, depending on what they are reacting with. These are called **amphiprotic** species. To be amphiprotic, a species must have a hydrogen atom it can donate, and it must also be able to accept a proton (which usually requires a lone pair of electrons).

items: Water ($H_2O$) is the classic example. It can donate a proton to become $OH^-$ or accept a proton to become $H_3O^+$.
The hydrogen carbonate ion, $HCO_3^-$, is another key example. It can donate its proton to form the carbonate ion, $CO_3^{2-}$, or accept a proton to form carbonic acid, $H_2CO_3$.
Other examples include the hydrogen sulfate ion ($HSO_4^-$) and dihydrogen phosphate ion ($H_2PO_4^-$).

sections.content: In exams, a common question is to write two different equations showing a species acting as an acid in one and a base in the other. Make sure you choose appropriate reaction partners. To show it as an acid, react it with a base (like $OH^-$). To show it as a base, react it with an acid (like $HCl$ or $H_3O^+$).

sections.question:
The hydrogen sulfate ion, $HSO_4^-$, is amphiprotic. 
(i) Write an equation for its reaction with water where it acts as an acid. 
(ii) Write an equation for its reaction with hydrofluoric acid, $HF$, where it acts as a base.
sections.solution:
**(i) Acting as an acid:**
When $HSO_4^-$ acts as an acid, it donates a proton. Water will act as the base.

$HSO_4^-(aq) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons SO_4^{2-}(aq) + H_3O^+(aq)$ [1]

*   Here, $HSO_4^-$ is the acid and its conjugate base is $SO_4^{2-}$.

**(ii) Acting as a base:**
When $HSO_4^-$ acts as a base, it accepts a proton. The stronger acid, $HF$, will be the proton donor.

$HSO_4^-(aq) + HF(aq) \rightleftharpoons H_2SO_4(aq) + F^-(aq)$ [1]

*   Here, $HSO_4^-$ is the base and its conjugate acid is $H_2SO_4$ (sulfuric acid).

sections.label: Test Your Knowledge on Proton Transfer
